guide.title: Complete Apache Airflow Guide for Data Engineers

topics.name: What is Apache Airflow
topics.explanation: Apache Airflow is an open-source platform for programmatically authoring, scheduling, and monitoring workflows. Created at Airbnb in 2014 by Maxime Beauchemin, donated to Apache Software Foundation in 2016, became a top-level Apache project in 2019. Workflows are defined as Python code — making them version-controllable, testable, and maintainable. Airflow is NOT a data streaming tool — it orchestrates tasks, not processes data in motion.
key_features: Rich UI for monitoring and managing pipelines
Built-in retry, alerting, and SLA tracking
Scalable from single machine to thousands of workers
Strong ecosystem of provider packages (AWS, GCP, Azure, Snowflake, dbt...)
what_airflow_is_not: Not a data streaming tool (use Kafka/Flink for that)
Not a data transformation tool (use Spark/dbt for that)
Not suitable for sub-minute scheduling (overhead per task run)
Not a replacement for event-driven architectures

example.simple_dag:
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from datetime import datetime

with DAG('my_first_dag', start_date=datetime(2024,1,1), schedule='@daily') as dag:
    def extract(): print('Extracting data...')
    def load():    print('Loading data...')
    t1 = PythonOperator(task_id='extract', python_callable=extract)
    t2 = PythonOperator(task_id='load',    python_callable=load)
    t1 >> t2

topics.name: Why Airflow (vs Cron, Luigi, Oozie)

comparison_table.airflow: Rich UI with DAG graph, logs, history

why_airflow_wins: Workflows as Python — version control, testing, reuse
Excellent UI — visualize pipelines, debug failures
Massive ecosystem — AWS, GCP, Azure, Spark, dbt all built-in
Active community — most popular orchestrator in data engineering

components.role: Serves the Airflow UI. Reads DAG metadata from the database. Does NOT execute tasks.
Heart of Airflow. Parses DAG files, schedules task instances, submits them to the executor. Runs continuously.
Determines HOW tasks are executed. Pluggable — Sequential, Local, Celery, Kubernetes.
Stores DAG definitions, task instances, connections, variables, XComs, logs metadata. PostgreSQL or MySQL recommended for production.
Folder containing Python DAG files. Scheduler parses this directory periodically.
Actual execution of tasks. In CeleryExecutor: Celery workers. In KubernetesExecutor: individual pods.
components.note: Not needed for SequentialExecutor or LocalExecutor

components.role: Queue for task messages between scheduler and workers.
Runs deferrable operators asynchronously. Single thread handles thousands of deferred tasks.

architecture_diagram.flow: DAG Files → Scheduler (parse) → Metadata DB (store) → Executor → Workers (execute) → Metadata DB (update status) → Webserver (display)

topics.name: Local Installation (pip)
topics.explanation: Install Airflow locally using pip with constraint files to ensure compatible package versions.

steps.command:
python -m venv airflow_venv
source airflow_venv/bin/activate  # Linux/Mac
airflow_venv\Scripts\activate      # Windows
airflow users create --username admin --password admin --firstname Admin --lastname User --role Admin --email admin@example.com

topics.name: Docker Compose Setup
topics.explanation: The official Airflow Docker Compose setup is the fastest way to get a production-like environment running locally.
topics.custom_dockerfile:
FROM apache/airflow:2.8.0
USER root
RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y --no-install-recommends vim
USER airflow
RUN pip install apache-airflow-providers-snowflake apache-airflow-providers-dbt-cloud

Any config can be overridden via environment variable: AIRFLOW__SECTION__KEY
Example: AIRFLOW__CORE__EXECUTOR=LocalExecutor

topics.name: What is a DAG
topics.explanation: A DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) is a collection of tasks organized with dependencies and relationships that define how they should run. Directed: edges have direction (task A must run before task B). Acyclic: no cycles — task cannot depend on itself directly or indirectly. Graph: nodes are tasks, edges are dependencies.
dag_properties: schedule: When the DAG runs (cron, timedelta, @daily, Dataset)
start_date: When the DAG's schedule begins
end_date: Optional — when the DAG's schedule ends
catchup: Whether to backfill missed runs (default: True)
max_active_runs: Max concurrent runs of this DAG
tags: List of string tags for filtering in UI
default_args: Default arguments applied to all tasks in DAG

example.full_dag:
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from airflow.operators.bash import BashOperator
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

default_args = {
    'owner': 'data-team',
    'retries': 3,
    'retry_delay': timedelta(minutes=5),
    'email': ['alerts@company.com'],
    'email_on_failure': True,
    'email_on_retry': False
}

with DAG(
    dag_id='sales_pipeline',
    description='Daily sales ETL pipeline',
    schedule='0 2 * * *',       # 2am daily
    start_date=datetime(2024, 1, 1),
    catchup=False,
    max_active_runs=1,
    tags=['sales', 'etl', 'daily'],
    default_args=default_args
) as dag:
    extract = BashOperator(task_id='extract', bash_command='python /scripts/extract.py')
    transform = PythonOperator(task_id='transform', python_callable=transform_fn)
    load = PythonOperator(task_id='load', python_callable=load_fn)
    extract >> transform >> load

topics.name: DAG Scheduling
topics.explanation: Airflow uses execution_date (logical date) — the start of the scheduled interval, NOT the actual run time. A DAG with schedule='@daily' and start_date=2024-01-01 will have its first run at 2024-01-02 00:00 covering the interval 2024-01-01.

examples: schedule=[my_dataset] — run when dataset is updated

topics.name: Catchup & Backfill
topics.explanation: Catchup: when a DAG is unpaused or start_date is in the past, Airflow schedules all missed runs. Backfill: manually trigger historical runs for a date range.

catchup_example.scenario: DAG with start_date=2024-01-01, schedule=@daily, catchup=True, activated on 2024-01-10
catchup_example.result: Airflow immediately schedules 9 missed runs: Jan 1 through Jan 9
catchup_example.disable: catchup=False in DAG definition — only run from current date forward

drawbacks: catchup=True can create hundreds of unexpected runs when activating old DAGs
Backfill runs share the same pool/concurrency limits as regular runs
solutions: Always set catchup=False for new DAGs unless backfill is intentional
Set max_active_runs=1 to prevent parallel execution of backfill and live runs

topics.name: DAG Decorators (@dag)
topics.explanation: The @dag decorator from the TaskFlow API provides a cleaner, more Pythonic way to define DAGs.

example.traditional:
with DAG('my_dag', schedule='@daily', start_date=datetime(2024,1,1)) as dag:
    task1 = PythonOperator(task_id='task1', python_callable=fn1)
    task2 = PythonOperator(task_id='task2', python_callable=fn2)
    task1 >> task2

practices.reason: Prevents unexpected backfill runs on activation
Prevents concurrent DAG runs for idempotent pipelines
Consistent retry behavior across all tasks
Scheduler parses DAG files every 30s — heavy imports slow everything

practices.practice: No dynamic imports in DAG file
practices.reason: DB calls or API calls at parse time crash the scheduler
Filter and organize DAGs in UI
Better visibility, easier retry on failure
Avoid datetime.now() in start_date — use a fixed date

topics.name: PythonOperator
topics.explanation: Executes a Python callable. Most commonly used operator. The callable can accept context variables via op_kwargs or **context.

example.basic:
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator

def process_data(date, **context):
    execution_date = context['ds']  # '2024-01-15'
    print(f'Processing data for {date} on {execution_date}')
    return {'rows_processed': 1000}

process_task = PythonOperator(
    task_id='process_data',
    python_callable=process_data,
    op_kwargs={'date': '{{ ds }}'},  # Jinja templated
    dag=dag
)
example.with_context:
def my_func(**context):
    ti = context['ti']               # TaskInstance
    ds = context['ds']               # execution date string: '2024-01-15'
    ds_nodash = context['ds_nodash'] # '20240115'
    prev_ds = context['prev_ds']     # previous execution date
    next_ds = context['next_ds']     # next execution date
    params = context['params']       # DAG/task params
    print(f'Run ID: {context["run_id"]}')

topics.name: BashOperator
topics.explanation: Executes a bash command or script. Useful for running shell scripts, CLI tools, Python scripts as subprocesses.

example.basic:
from airflow.operators.bash import BashOperator

run_script = BashOperator(
    task_id='run_etl_script',
    bash_command='python /opt/scripts/etl.py --date {{ ds }} --env prod',
    env={'DB_HOST': 'localhost', 'DB_PORT': '5432'},
    cwd='/opt/scripts'
)
example.multiline:
run_sql = BashOperator(
    task_id='run_sql',
    bash_command="""
        psql -h $DB_HOST -U $DB_USER -d mydb -c \
        "INSERT INTO summary SELECT date, SUM(amount) FROM orders WHERE date='{{ ds }}' GROUP BY date"
    """
)

drawbacks: Environment differs from developer machine — PATH, env vars may be missing
Exit code != 0 raises AirflowException — scripts must handle errors and exit codes

topics.name: BranchPythonOperator
topics.explanation: Executes a Python callable that RETURNS a task_id (or list of task_ids) to run next. All other downstream branches are skipped.

example.code:
from airflow.operators.python import BranchPythonOperator
from airflow.operators.empty import EmptyOperator

def choose_branch(**context):
    day_of_week = context['execution_date'].weekday()  # 0=Mon, 6=Sun
    if day_of_week == 0:  # Monday
        return 'weekly_report'
    elif day_of_week == 6:  # Sunday
        return ['sunday_cleanup', 'send_weekly_summary']  # multiple branches
    else:
        return 'daily_report'

branch = BranchPythonOperator(
    task_id='check_day',
    python_callable=choose_branch
)
daily_report  = EmptyOperator(task_id='daily_report')
weekly_report = EmptyOperator(task_id='weekly_report')
sunday_cleanup= EmptyOperator(task_id='sunday_cleanup')
send_summary  = EmptyOperator(task_id='send_weekly_summary')

branch >> [daily_report, weekly_report, sunday_cleanup, send_summary]
example.note: Skipped tasks show in UI with grey color. Tasks downstream of skipped tasks are also skipped unless trigger_rule is set to 'none_failed_min_one_success'.

topics.name: ShortCircuitOperator
topics.explanation: Executes a Python callable. If it returns False, all downstream tasks are SKIPPED. If True, pipeline continues normally.

example.code:
from airflow.operators.python import ShortCircuitOperator

def check_data_exists(**context):
    import boto3
    s3 = boto3.client('s3')
    try:
        s3.head_object(Bucket='my-bucket', Key=f"data/{context['ds']}/orders.csv")
        return True   # File exists — continue
    except:
        return False  # File missing — skip all downstream tasks

check_file = ShortCircuitOperator(
    task_id='check_file_exists',
    python_callable=check_data_exists,
    ignore_downstream_trigger_rules=True  # skip ALL downstream
)
example.use_case: Skip pipeline when no new data arrived. Skip weekend runs in a daily pipeline.

topics.name: TriggerDagRunOperator
topics.explanation: Triggers another DAG from within a DAG. Useful for parent-child DAG patterns or event-driven DAG triggering.

example.code:
from airflow.operators.trigger_dagrun import TriggerDagRunOperator

trigger_child = TriggerDagRunOperator(
    task_id='trigger_reporting_dag',
    trigger_dag_id='reporting_pipeline',
    conf={'parent_run_id': '{{ run_id }}', 'date': '{{ ds }}'},
    wait_for_completion=True,   # Wait for child DAG to finish
    poke_interval=30,           # Check every 30 seconds
    reset_dag_run=True          # Re-run if child already ran for this date
)

topics.name: Custom Operators
topics.explanation: Create custom operators by extending BaseOperator and implementing the execute() method.

example.code:
from airflow.models import BaseOperator
from airflow.utils.decorators import apply_defaults
from typing import Any

class SnowflakeToS3Operator(BaseOperator):
    """
    Custom operator: exports Snowflake query results to S3 as Parquet
    """
    template_fields = ('sql', 's3_key')  # These fields support Jinja templating

    def __init__(
        self,
        sql: str,
        s3_bucket: str,
        s3_key: str,
        snowflake_conn_id: str = 'snowflake_default',
        aws_conn_id: str = 'aws_default',
        **kwargs
    ):
        super().__init__(**kwargs)
        self.sql = sql
        self.s3_bucket = s3_bucket
        self.s3_key = s3_key
        self.snowflake_conn_id = snowflake_conn_id
        self.aws_conn_id = aws_conn_id

    def execute(self, context: Any):
        from airflow.providers.snowflake.hooks.snowflake import SnowflakeHook
        from airflow.providers.amazon.aws.hooks.s3 import S3Hook
        import pandas as pd

        self.log.info(f'Running query: {self.sql}')
        sf_hook = SnowflakeHook(snowflake_conn_id=self.snowflake_conn_id)
        df = sf_hook.get_pandas_df(self.sql)

        s3_hook = S3Hook(aws_conn_id=self.aws_conn_id)
        local_path = f'/tmp/export_{context["ds_nodash"]}.parquet'
        df.to_parquet(local_path, index=False)
        s3_hook.load_file(local_path, self.s3_key, self.s3_bucket, replace=True)
        self.log.info(f'Exported {len(df)} rows to s3://{self.s3_bucket}/{self.s3_key}')
        return len(df)

# Usage:
export_task = SnowflakeToS3Operator(
    task_id='export_daily_sales',
    sql="SELECT * FROM sales WHERE date = '{{ ds }}'",
    s3_bucket='my-data-lake',
    s3_key='exports/sales/{{ ds_nodash }}/sales.parquet'
)

topics.name: @task Decorator
topics.explanation: The @task decorator wraps a Python function as an Airflow task automatically. Return values are automatically pushed to XCom. Input arguments from other @task functions are automatically pulled from XCom.

topics.name: @task.branch
topics.explanation: Branching equivalent in TaskFlow API. Return the task_id(s) to execute.

comparison_table.taskflow: Automatic — return value = XCom push, arg = XCom pull

comparison_table.traditional: More explicit — better for complex dependency graphs

comparison_table.taskflow: Cannot use for BashOperator, Sensors etc.
Python-centric pipelines, data passing between tasks
comparison_table.traditional: When using non-Python operators or complex DAG structures

topics.name: Mixed TaskFlow and Traditional Operators
topics.explanation: TaskFlow and traditional operators can be freely mixed in the same DAG.

topics.name: What are Sensors
topics.explanation: Sensors are special operators that wait for a condition to be true before proceeding. They periodically check the condition (poke) until it's met or times out. Common use: wait for a file to arrive, an API to return data, an external task to complete.

base_sensor_params.description: Max seconds to wait before failing (7 days default)
'poke' (holds worker slot) or 'reschedule' (releases slot between checks)
If True, mark task as SKIPPED instead of FAILED on timeout
Exponentially increase poke_interval on each check

topics.name: Poke vs Reschedule Mode
topics.explanation: Critical performance difference. Poke mode holds the worker slot for the entire wait duration. Reschedule mode releases the worker slot between checks — much more efficient for long waits.

poke_mode.behavior: Sensor runs continuously, sleeping between checks. Holds a worker slot the ENTIRE time.
poke_mode.use_when: Short waits (< 5 minutes). High poke frequency needed.
poke_mode.drawback: With 10 sensors poking for 2 hours each = 10 worker slots blocked for 2 hours

reschedule_mode.behavior: Sensor checks condition, then RELEASES the worker slot until next check time. Scheduler re-queues it.
reschedule_mode.use_when: Long waits (> 5 minutes). Default for most production sensors.
reschedule_mode.benefit: 10 sensors in reschedule mode use near-zero worker slots between checks

topics.example:
from airflow.providers.amazon.aws.sensors.s3 import S3KeySensor

# BAD for long waits — holds worker slot
wait_poke = S3KeySensor(
    task_id='wait_poke',
    mode='poke',           # Holds worker for entire wait
    poke_interval=60,
    timeout=3600
)

# GOOD for long waits — releases worker between checks
wait_reschedule = S3KeySensor(
    task_id='wait_reschedule',
    mode='reschedule',     # Releases worker between checks
    poke_interval=300,     # Check every 5 minutes
    timeout=7200           # Max 2 hours
)

topics.name: S3KeySensor
topics.explanation: Waits for a file (key) to exist in an S3 bucket.

example.single_file:
from airflow.providers.amazon.aws.sensors.s3 import S3KeySensor

wait_for_orders = S3KeySensor(
    task_id='wait_for_orders',
    bucket_name='my-data-lake',
    bucket_key='raw/orders/{{ ds }}/orders.csv',
    aws_conn_id='aws_default',
    mode='reschedule',
    poke_interval=300,
    timeout=7200
)
example.wildcard:
wait_any_file = S3KeySensor(
    task_id='wait_any_parquet',
    bucket_name='my-data-lake',
    bucket_key='raw/orders/{{ ds }}/*.parquet',
    wildcard_match=True,   # Use glob pattern
    mode='reschedule'
)

topics.name: ExternalTaskSensor
topics.explanation: Waits for a task or entire DAG in another (or same) DAG to complete successfully. The most common way to create cross-DAG dependencies.

example.code:
from airflow.sensors.external_task import ExternalTaskSensor
from datetime import timedelta

# Wait for 'extract' task in 'upstream_pipeline' DAG
wait_upstream = ExternalTaskSensor(
    task_id='wait_for_upstream',
    external_dag_id='upstream_pipeline',
    external_task_id='extract',        # None = wait for entire DAG
    execution_delta=timedelta(hours=1),# upstream runs 1 hour before this DAG
    mode='reschedule',
    poke_interval=60,
    timeout=3600,
    check_existence=True               # Fail fast if upstream DAG doesn't exist
)
example.execution_date_fn:
# Custom function for non-standard schedule alignments
wait_upstream = ExternalTaskSensor(
    task_id='wait_for_upstream',
    external_dag_id='hourly_pipeline',
    execution_date_fn=lambda dt: dt.replace(hour=0, minute=0)  # Always wait for midnight run
)

drawbacks: Tight coupling between DAGs by dag_id string
Hard to detect if upstream DAG was renamed or deleted (check_existence helps)
execution_delta must be carefully calibrated

topics.name: Custom Sensors
topics.explanation: Create custom sensors by extending BaseSensorOperator and implementing the poke() method. Return True when condition is met.

example.code:
from airflow.sensors.base import BaseSensorOperator
from typing import Any

class TableRowCountSensor(BaseSensorOperator):
    """
    Waits until a database table has at least min_rows rows for a given date.
    """
    template_fields = ('date', 'table_name')

    def __init__(
        self,
        table_name: str,
        date: str,
        min_rows: int = 1,
        postgres_conn_id: str = 'postgres_default',
        **kwargs
    ):
        super().__init__(**kwargs)
        self.table_name = table_name
        self.date = date
        self.min_rows = min_rows
        self.postgres_conn_id = postgres_conn_id

    def poke(self, context: Any) -> bool:
        from airflow.providers.postgres.hooks.postgres import PostgresHook
        hook = PostgresHook(postgres_conn_id=self.postgres_conn_id)
        sql = f"SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {self.table_name} WHERE date = '{self.date}'"
        count = hook.get_first(sql)[0]
        self.log.info(f'Row count for {self.date}: {count} (need {self.min_rows})')
        return count >= self.min_rows

# Usage:
wait_for_data = TableRowCountSensor(
    task_id='wait_for_orders',
    table_name='orders',
    date='{{ ds }}',
    min_rows=100,
    mode='reschedule',
    poke_interval=120
)

topics.name: What is XCom
topics.explanation: XCom (Cross-Communication) allows tasks to exchange small amounts of data. Data is stored in Airflow's metadata database. When a task pushes a value, it's stored with a key and the task's identity. Other tasks can pull it by specifying the source task_id and key.

xcom_limits.recommendation: XCom is designed for SMALL data: IDs, counts, file paths, status flags. NOT for DataFrames, large datasets.
xcom_limits.rule_of_thumb: If value > 1KB, store data externally (S3, DB) and pass the reference path via XCom

topics.name: XCom Push & Pull
topics.explanation: Push: store value in XCom. Pull: retrieve value from XCom.

example.traditional_push_pull:
def extract(**context):
    data_path = 's3://bucket/data/{{ ds }}/orders.parquet'
    # Method 1: Return value (auto-pushed with key='return_value')
    return data_path

    # Method 2: Explicit push with custom key
    # context['ti'].xcom_push(key='orders_path', value=data_path)

def transform(**context):
    ti = context['ti']
    # Pull return value from previous task
    path = ti.xcom_pull(task_ids='extract')
    # Pull specific key
    # path = ti.xcom_pull(task_ids='extract', key='orders_path')
    print(f'Processing file: {path}')
example.multiple_pulls:
def report(**context):
    ti = context['ti']
    # Pull from multiple tasks
    extract_count = ti.xcom_pull(task_ids='extract', key='row_count')
    load_count    = ti.xcom_pull(task_ids='load',    key='return_value')
    print(f'Extracted: {extract_count}, Loaded: {load_count}')
example.in_template:
# XCom can be accessed in Jinja templates
run_sql = BashOperator(
    task_id='use_xcom',
    bash_command="echo Processing file: {{ ti.xcom_pull(task_ids='extract') }}"
)

topics.name: Custom XCom Backends
topics.explanation: For larger data, use a custom XCom backend that stores data in S3/GCS instead of the metadata DB.

topics.name: XCom Best Practices
practices: Pass file paths or S3 URIs, not file contents
Pass record counts, status codes, metadata — not actual datasets
Use TaskFlow API for automatic XCom management
Set xcom_push=False on operators that produce large return values you don't need
Clean up old XCom entries: airflow db clean --clean-before-timestamp 2024-01-01
For large data transfer: write to S3/GCS, pass the path via XCom

topics.name: What are Connections
topics.explanation: Connections store credentials and connection details for external systems (databases, APIs, cloud services). Stored encrypted in the metadata DB using Fernet encryption. Referenced by conn_id in operators and hooks.
connection_fields: Conn Type: Type of connection (postgres, mysql, http, aws, etc.)
Extra: JSON with additional config (e.g., role, warehouse for Snowflake)

methods.description: Admin → Connections → Add Connection. Easiest for development.
methods.drawback: Not version-controlled, not suitable for production

methods.benefit: Version-controlled via .env files or K8s secrets

methods.description: Store connections in AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or GCP Secret Manager. Best for production.

topics.name: What are Hooks
topics.explanation: Hooks are interfaces to external platforms and databases, built on top of Connections. They abstract the authentication and communication details. Operators use Hooks internally.

example.postgres_hook:
from airflow.providers.postgres.hooks.postgres import PostgresHook

def run_query(**context):
    hook = PostgresHook(postgres_conn_id='postgres_dwh')

    # Execute SQL
    hook.run('INSERT INTO audit_log VALUES (%(date)s, %(count)s)',
             parameters={'date': context['ds'], 'count': 100})

    # Get results as list of tuples
    rows = hook.get_records('SELECT id, name FROM customers LIMIT 10')

    # Get first row
    row = hook.get_first('SELECT COUNT(*) FROM orders')

    # Get pandas DataFrame
    df = hook.get_pandas_df('SELECT * FROM orders WHERE date = %(date)s',
                            parameters={'date': context['ds']})
    return len(df)
example.s3_hook:
from airflow.providers.amazon.aws.hooks.s3 import S3Hook

def upload_to_s3(**context):
    hook = S3Hook(aws_conn_id='aws_default')

    # Upload file
    hook.load_file(
        filename='/tmp/orders.parquet',
        key=f"data/{context['ds']}/orders.parquet",
        bucket_name='my-data-lake',
        replace=True
    )

    # Check if key exists
    exists = hook.check_for_key('data/2024-01-15/orders.parquet', 'my-data-lake')

    # List keys
    keys = hook.list_keys(bucket_name='my-data-lake', prefix='data/2024-01-15/')
    return keys

topics.name: Custom Hooks
topics.explanation: Create custom hooks by extending BaseHook for any external system not covered by existing providers.

topics.name: Airflow Variables
topics.explanation: Variables are key-value pairs stored in Airflow's metadata DB. Used for configuration values that change between environments (dev/staging/prod). Unlike connections, variables store simple config, not credentials.

example.setting_variables:
# Via UI: Admin → Variables → Add
# Via CLI:
airflow variables set s3_bucket 'my-data-lake-prod'
airflow variables set max_retries '3'
airflow variables set config '{"env": "prod", "region": "us-east-1"}'  # JSON
example.reading_in_dag:
from airflow.models import Variable

# Single variable
bucket = Variable.get('s3_bucket')  # 'my-data-lake-prod'

# With default (doesn't fail if missing)
bucket = Variable.get('s3_bucket', default_var='fallback-bucket')

# JSON variable (auto-deserializes)
config = Variable.get('config', deserialize_json=True)
env = config['env']  # 'prod'

drawbacks: Variable.get() at DAG parse time causes extra DB queries on every scheduler heartbeat
Hardcoding variable names as strings is fragile
solutions: Access Variables inside tasks (inside callables), not at DAG parse time
Use os.environ for environment-specific config to avoid DB queries at parse
Use Jinja templating {{ var.value.key }} which is evaluated at task execution, not parse time

topics.name: Jinja Templating
topics.explanation: Airflow uses Jinja2 templating to dynamically inject values into task parameters. Templates are rendered at task execution time (not DAG parse time).

example.code:
# Template fields are rendered at task execution
extract = BashOperator(
    task_id='extract',
    bash_command='''
        python extract.py \
          --date {{ ds }} \
          --prev-date {{ prev_ds }} \
          --bucket {{ var.value.s3_bucket }} \
          --env {{ var.json.config.env }} \
          --run-id {{ run_id }}
    '''
)

# Making custom fields templateable in custom operators:
class MyOperator(BaseOperator):
    template_fields = ('sql', 'output_path')  # These fields support Jinja
    sql: str
    output_path: str

topics.name: Macros
topics.explanation: Macros are functions and objects available in Jinja templates. Airflow provides built-in macros and you can register custom ones.

built_in_macros.description: Add N days to date string

topics.example:
# Get date 7 days ago:
bash_command = 'python script.py --start {{ macros.ds_add(ds, -7) }} --end {{ ds }}'

# Get first day of month:
bash_command = "python script.py --month {{ ds[:7] }}-01"

# Custom macro registered in DAG:
my_dag = DAG(
    'my_dag',
    user_defined_macros={
        'my_function': lambda x: x.upper(),
        'env': 'production'
    }
)
# Usage: {{ my_function(ds) }}  → '2024-01-15'

topics.name: SequentialExecutor
topics.explanation: Runs one task at a time sequentially. Uses SQLite as backend. Only for local development and testing — NEVER for production.
limitations: Single task at a time — no parallelism
topics.use_case: Local development, CI/CD testing with simple DAGs

topics.name: LocalExecutor
topics.explanation: Runs tasks in parallel using Python multiprocessing on the same machine as the scheduler. Simple, no external dependencies. Good for small-medium workloads.
topics.parallelism: Limited by machine's CPU and memory
topics.best_for: Single server deployments, small teams, up to ~50 concurrent tasks
drawbacks: Single point of failure
Limited by one machine's resources
solutions: Use CeleryExecutor or KubernetesExecutor for horizontal scaling

topics.name: CeleryExecutor
topics.explanation: Distributes tasks across multiple worker machines using Celery task queue. Most popular for production medium-to-large scale deployments.

components: Scheduler: queues task messages to broker
Broker (Redis/RabbitMQ): message queue between scheduler and workers
Workers: pull tasks from broker and execute
architecture.scaling: Add/remove worker machines dynamically. Each worker can run multiple tasks in parallel.

topics.config:
executor = CeleryExecutor
broker_url = redis://redis:6379/0
result_backend = db+postgresql://user:pass@postgres/airflow
worker_concurrency = 16

commands.scale_workers:
# Start N additional workers:
for i in {1..5}; do airflow celery worker -D; done
commands.monitor: airflow celery flower  # Web UI for Celery monitoring at :5555

drawbacks: Additional infrastructure (Redis/RabbitMQ)
All workers need same DAG code and dependencies installed
Worker environments can drift over time
solutions: Use Docker containers for workers — guaranteed consistent environment
Use KubernetesExecutor if environment isolation is critical

topics.name: KubernetesExecutor
topics.explanation: Launches each task as a separate Kubernetes Pod. Full environment isolation per task. No idle workers. Tasks start from scratch for each run.

architecture.flow: Scheduler → Kubernetes API → Pod created → Task runs → Pod terminated → Status reported to metadata DB

topics.config:
executor = KubernetesExecutor
[kubernetes]
namespace = airflow
worker_container_repository = my-registry/airflow
worker_container_tag = 2.8.0
pod_template_file = /opt/airflow/pod_template.yaml
topics.pod_template_example:
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
spec:
  containers:
    - name: base
      image: my-registry/airflow:2.8.0
      resources:
        requests:
          memory: '2Gi'
          cpu: '500m'
        limits:
          memory: '4Gi'
          cpu: '2000m'
      env:
        - name: AIRFLOW__CORE__SQL_ALCHEMY_CONN
          valueFrom:
            secretKeyRef:
              name: airflow-secrets
              key: sql-alchemy-conn
advantages: No idle workers — zero cost when no tasks running
Easy dependency management (custom Docker images per task)
drawbacks: Pod startup overhead (30-60 seconds) — not suitable for sub-minute tasks
Kubernetes expertise required
Higher complexity than CeleryExecutor

topics.name: Bitshift Operators & Dependency Methods
topics.explanation: Define task execution order using >> (downstream) and << (upstream) operators, or explicit methods.

example.bitshift:
# >> means 'then'
extract >> transform >> load

# << means 'after'
load << transform << extract

# Multiple dependencies:
extract >> [transform_a, transform_b] >> load

# Fan-in:
[transform_a, transform_b] >> load
example.explicit_methods:
# Equivalent to extract >> transform:
transform.set_upstream(extract)
extract.set_downstream(transform)

# Chain helper:
from airflow.models.baseoperator import chain
chain(extract, transform, load)

# Cross downstream:
from airflow.models.baseoperator import cross_downstream
cross_downstream([a, b], [c, d])  # a>>c, a>>d, b>>c, b>>d

topics.name: Trigger Rules
topics.explanation: Trigger rules define WHEN a task should run based on its upstream tasks' states. Default is 'all_success' — task runs only when all upstream tasks succeed.

trigger_rules.description: Run when ALL upstream tasks succeed
Run when ALL upstream tasks have failed
trigger_rules.use_case: Cleanup or alerting on total failure

trigger_rules.description: Run when ALL upstream tasks are done (any state: success, failed, skipped)
trigger_rules.use_case: Final cleanup task that runs regardless of outcome

trigger_rules.description: Run when AT LEAST ONE upstream task succeeds
trigger_rules.use_case: Run as soon as any parallel branch completes

trigger_rules.description: Run when AT LEAST ONE upstream task fails
trigger_rules.use_case: Alert as soon as any task fails

trigger_rules.description: Run when no upstream task has failed or upstream_failed (skipped is OK)
trigger_rules.use_case: After branching — run when branch was skipped but not failed

trigger_rules.description: Run when no failures AND at least one success (handles branches)
Run when no upstream task was skipped

topics.branch_example:
branch_task = BranchPythonOperator(...)
path_a = PythonOperator(task_id='path_a', ...)
path_b = PythonOperator(task_id='path_b', ...)

# Final task uses none_failed_min_one_success to handle skipped branches
final = PythonOperator(
    task_id='final',
    trigger_rule='none_failed_min_one_success',
    python_callable=finalize
)

branch_task >> [path_a, path_b] >> final

topics.name: Depends on Past
topics.explanation: When depends_on_past=True, a task instance will only run if the previous run of the same task succeeded (or was skipped). Ensures sequential processing of dated data.

example.code:
process_data = PythonOperator(
    task_id='process_data',
    python_callable=process,
    depends_on_past=True   # Won't run if yesterday's run failed
)
example.use_case: Incremental loads where today's data depends on yesterday's being processed correctly
example.drawback: Can cause pipeline to halt entirely if one day fails — all future days wait
example.solution: Fix the failing run and mark it as success, or clear it for retry

topics.name: Wait for Downstream
topics.explanation: When wait_for_downstream=True on a task, it waits for the downstream tasks of the PREVIOUS run to complete before this run starts. Ensures downstream tasks of the previous interval are done.
topics.use_case: When a load task writes partitioned data and downstream reporting needs previous partition complete before this run's extract starts

topics.name: TaskGroups
topics.explanation: TaskGroups visually group related tasks in the Airflow UI without creating a separate DAG. They are the modern replacement for SubDAGs. TaskGroups are purely visual — they don't change execution behavior. They collapse in the UI for cleaner DAG graphs.

example.code:
from airflow.utils.task_group import TaskGroup
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from airflow.decorators import dag
from datetime import datetime

@dag(schedule='@daily', start_date=datetime(2024,1,1), catchup=False)
def grouped_pipeline():

    with TaskGroup('extract_group', tooltip='All extraction tasks') as extract_grp:
        extract_orders   = PythonOperator(task_id='extract_orders',   python_callable=lambda: None)
        extract_customers= PythonOperator(task_id='extract_customers', python_callable=lambda: None)
        extract_products = PythonOperator(task_id='extract_products',  python_callable=lambda: None)
        [extract_orders, extract_customers, extract_products]  # run in parallel

    with TaskGroup('transform_group') as transform_grp:
        clean_orders   = PythonOperator(task_id='clean_orders',   python_callable=lambda: None)
        clean_customers= PythonOperator(task_id='clean_customers', python_callable=lambda: None)
        join_data      = PythonOperator(task_id='join_data',       python_callable=lambda: None)
        [clean_orders, clean_customers] >> join_data

    with TaskGroup('load_group') as load_grp:
        load_warehouse = PythonOperator(task_id='load_warehouse', python_callable=lambda: None)
        load_datamart  = PythonOperator(task_id='load_datamart',  python_callable=lambda: None)

    extract_grp >> transform_grp >> load_grp

grouped_pipeline()

nested_task_groups.code:
with TaskGroup('etl') as etl:
    with TaskGroup('extract', parent_group=etl) as extract:
        t1 = EmptyOperator(task_id='orders')
        t2 = EmptyOperator(task_id='products')
    with TaskGroup('load', parent_group=etl) as load:
        t3 = EmptyOperator(task_id='to_warehouse')
    extract >> load

topics.name: SubDAGs (legacy)
topics.explanation: SubDAGs create a child DAG that appears as a single task in the parent DAG. Deprecated in Airflow 2.x — TaskGroups are the modern replacement.
why_deprecated: SubDAGs use SubDagOperator which spawns a separate scheduler run
Causes deadlocks with CeleryExecutor (child tasks use parent's pool slots)
Complex to debug — two separate DAG UIs
Performance overhead of spawning child scheduler
topics.migration: Replace SubDAGOperator with TaskGroup — same visual grouping, better performance, no deadlocks

topics.name: Dynamic DAG Generation
topics.explanation: Generate multiple DAGs programmatically from a config file, database, or list. Each generated DAG is a full DAG object registered with Airflow.

example.from_config:
# config/clients.yaml:
# clients:
#   - name: acme_corp
#     schedule: '0 2 * * *'
#     s3_bucket: acme-data
#   - name: globex
#     schedule: '0 4 * * *'
#     s3_bucket: globex-data

import yaml
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from datetime import datetime

with open('/opt/airflow/config/clients.yaml') as f:
    clients = yaml.safe_load(f)['clients']

for client in clients:
    dag_id = f"ingest_{client['name']}"

    with DAG(
        dag_id=dag_id,
        schedule=client['schedule'],
        start_date=datetime(2024, 1, 1),
        catchup=False,
        tags=['client-ingestion', client['name']]
    ) as dag:

        def extract(bucket=client['s3_bucket'], **ctx):
            print(f'Extracting from s3://{bucket}/{ctx["ds"]}')

        def load(client_name=client['name'], **ctx):
            print(f'Loading data for client: {client_name}')

        t1 = PythonOperator(task_id='extract', python_callable=extract)
        t2 = PythonOperator(task_id='load',    python_callable=load)
        t1 >> t2

    globals()[dag_id] = dag  # Register each DAG globally

drawbacks: Too many generated DAGs slow down the Airflow scheduler (file parsing overhead)
Hard to debug when generated DAG logic is wrong
Scheduler must re-parse config file on every heartbeat
solutions: Cache config reads outside the DAG loop
Use Dynamic Task Mapping instead if all DAGs share same structure

topics.name: Dynamic Task Mapping (.expand())
topics.explanation: Dynamic Task Mapping (Airflow 2.3+) creates multiple instances of the same task at runtime based on a list of inputs. No need to know the number of tasks at DAG write time. Each mapped task instance runs independently.
drawbacks: Mapped task count not known until runtime — UI shows [N mapped tasks] until run
All mapped task instances share the same pool slot settings
Cannot currently mix expand() with manual task instances easily

topics.name: What are Pools
topics.explanation: Pools limit the number of concurrently running tasks that can access a shared resource. When a task is assigned to a pool, it uses one slot. When the pool is full, tasks queue until a slot is released. Prevents overloading databases, APIs, or other limited resources.

example.create_pool:
# Via UI: Admin → Pools → Add Pool
# Via CLI:
airflow pools set snowflake_pool 5 'Max 5 concurrent Snowflake tasks'
airflow pools set postgres_pool 3 'Max 3 concurrent Postgres tasks'
airflow pools list
example.assign_task:
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator

# Task uses 1 slot from snowflake_pool
query_snowflake = PythonOperator(
    task_id='query_snowflake',
    python_callable=run_query,
    pool='snowflake_pool',         # Assigned pool
    pool_slots=1                   # Number of slots this task uses (default 1)
)

# Heavy task uses 2 slots
heavy_task = PythonOperator(
    task_id='heavy_snowflake_job',
    python_callable=run_heavy_query,
    pool='snowflake_pool',
    pool_slots=2                   # Takes 2 of the 5 available slots
)

use_cases: Limit concurrent connections to a database
Rate limit API calls to external services
Prevent too many Spark jobs submitting simultaneously
Control resource usage during business hours vs off-hours
drawbacks: Pool exhaustion causes tasks to queue indefinitely without clear error
Wrong pool_slots assignment can block other tasks
solutions: Monitor pool utilization via Airflow UI (Admin → Pools) or Prometheus metrics
Set task timeout to prevent tasks holding slots indefinitely
Alert on pool queue depth via custom metrics

topics.name: Priority Weights
topics.explanation: When multiple tasks are queued, priority_weight determines which runs first. Higher weight = higher priority. Used with WeightRule to compute effective priority.

example.code:
# Critical revenue pipeline runs first
revenue_task = PythonOperator(
    task_id='revenue_report',
    python_callable=compute_revenue,
    priority_weight=100,            # High priority
    weight_rule='absolute'          # Use this task's weight only
)

# Low priority background job
cleanup_task = PythonOperator(
    task_id='cleanup_old_files',
    python_callable=cleanup,
    priority_weight=1               # Low priority
)

weight_rules.downstream: Default. Weight = own weight + sum of all downstream task weights. Tasks closer to the end of pipeline get higher priority.
weight_rules.upstream: Weight = own weight + sum of all upstream task weights
weight_rules.absolute: Weight = exactly the value set. No propagation.

topics.name: Concurrency Settings
topics.explanation: Multiple layered settings control how many tasks run simultaneously.

settings.description: Global max tasks running across ALL DAGs simultaneously
Max concurrent tasks for any single DAG
Max concurrent DAG runs for any single DAG
Override max_active_tasks_per_dag for a specific DAG
Override max_active_runs_per_dag for a specific DAG
Controls concurrency for specific resource pools

topics.name: SLA Miss Callbacks
topics.explanation: SLAs define expected task/DAG completion time. If a task hasn't finished within its SLA, Airflow triggers the sla_miss_callback and sends email alerts.

example.code:
from airflow import DAG
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from datetime import datetime, timedelta

def sla_miss_alert(dag, task_list, blocking_task_list, slas, blocking_tis):
    """Called when SLA is missed"""
    message = f"SLA MISSED in DAG: {dag.dag_id}\n"
    message += f"Missed tasks: {[t.task_id for t in task_list]}\n"
    message += f"Blocking tasks: {[t.task_id for t in blocking_task_list]}"
    # Send to Slack/PagerDuty
    send_slack_alert(message)
    print(message)

with DAG(
    'critical_pipeline',
    schedule='0 6 * * *',
    start_date=datetime(2024,1,1),
    sla_miss_callback=sla_miss_alert,    # Called on SLA miss
    default_args={
        'sla': timedelta(hours=2)         # Each task must complete within 2 hours
    }
) as dag:
    extract = PythonOperator(
        task_id='extract',
        python_callable=extract_fn,
        sla=timedelta(hours=1)            # Override: this task must finish in 1 hour
    )

drawbacks: SLA is measured from DAG start time (execution_date), not task start time
SLA emails are sent only once — not repeatedly if task is still running
SLA miss does NOT stop the task — it just alerts

topics.name: Failure & Success Callbacks
topics.explanation: Callbacks triggered on specific task state changes. Applied at task or DAG level.

example.task_callbacks:
import requests

def on_failure(context):
    dag_id  = context['dag'].dag_id
    task_id = context['task_instance'].task_id
    exec_dt = context['ds']
    log_url = context['task_instance'].log_url
    exception = context.get('exception')

    message = (
        f":red_circle: *TASK FAILED*\n"
        f"DAG: `{dag_id}` | Task: `{task_id}`\n"
        f"Date: {exec_dt}\n"
        f"Error: {exception}\n"
        f"Logs: {log_url}"
    )
    requests.post(
        'https://hooks.slack.com/services/XXX/YYY/ZZZ',
        json={'text': message}
    )

def on_success(context):
    print(f"Task {context['task_instance'].task_id} succeeded!")

def on_retry(context):
    attempt = context['task_instance'].try_number
    print(f"Retry attempt {attempt}")

# Apply to specific task
risky_task = PythonOperator(
    task_id='risky_task',
    python_callable=fn,
    on_failure_callback=on_failure,
    on_success_callback=on_success,
    on_retry_callback=on_retry
)

# Apply to all tasks via default_args
default_args = {
    'on_failure_callback': on_failure,
    'retries': 3,
    'retry_delay': timedelta(minutes=5)
}

topics.name: Airflow Metrics (Prometheus & StatsD)
topics.explanation: Airflow emits metrics via StatsD. Use prometheus-statsd-exporter to convert to Prometheus format, then visualize in Grafana.

key_metrics.description: Time to parse all DAG files
DAG run duration for successful runs
DAG run duration for failed runs

topics.config:
# airflow.cfg:
[metrics]
statsd_on = True
statsd_host = statsd-host
statsd_port = 8125
statsd_prefix = airflow

topics.name: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
topics.explanation: Airflow uses Flask-AppBuilder RBAC to control who can see and do what in the UI. Roles have permissions on resources.

built_in_roles.permissions: Can view, trigger, and clear DAGs. Cannot manage users.
Can view and trigger DAGs. No admin actions.
Read-only. Can view DAGs and task logs.

example.create_user:
airflow users create \
  --username john \
  --password securepass \
  --firstname John \
  --lastname Doe \
  --role User \
  --email john@company.com
example.custom_role:
# Create custom role via UI: Security → List Roles → Add
# Or via Python:
from airflow.www.security import AirflowSecurityManager
# Custom role: can only view specific DAG
# Grant: can_read on DAG[sales_pipeline]

topics.name: Secrets Backend
topics.explanation: Store connections and variables in external secret managers (AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, GCP Secret Manager) instead of Airflow's metadata DB.

aws_secrets_manager.store_connection:
# AWS CLI: store connection
aws secretsmanager create-secret \
  --name airflow/connections/postgres_dwh \
  --secret-string '{"conn_type": "postgresql", "host": "mydb.rds.amazonaws.com", "schema": "analytics", "login": "airflow", "password": "secret", "port": 5432}'

topics.search_path: Airflow searches for secrets in order: 1) Secrets backend, 2) Environment variables, 3) Metadata DB

topics.name: Fernet Encryption
topics.explanation: Airflow uses Fernet symmetric encryption to encrypt connection passwords and variable values stored in the metadata DB.
topics.setup:
# Generate a Fernet key:
python -c "from cryptography.fernet import Fernet; print(Fernet.generate_key().decode())"
# Output: 'ZmDfcTF7_60GrrY167zsiPd67pEvs0aGOv2oasOM1Pg='

# Set in airflow.cfg:
[core]
fernet_key = ZmDfcTF7_60GrrY167zsiPd67pEvs0aGOv2oasOM1Pg=

# Or environment variable:
export AIRFLOW__CORE__FERNET_KEY='ZmDfcTF7_60GrrY167zsiPd67pEvs0aGOv2oasOM1Pg='
topics.key_rotation:
# Rotate key without losing old encrypted data:
airflow rotate-fernet-key  # Re-encrypts all connections/variables with new key
drawbacks: If Fernet key is lost, all encrypted connections/variables are unrecoverable
Same key across all Airflow instances — must be securely distributed
solutions: Store Fernet key in AWS Secrets Manager or Vault
Rotate keys periodically and immediately after team member departure

topics.name: Unit Testing DAGs
topics.explanation: Test DAG structure, task count, dependencies, and default args without executing tasks.

topics.name: Testing Operators & Callables
topics.explanation: Test the actual Python callable functions independently from Airflow infrastructure.

topics.name: CI/CD Pipeline for DAGs
topics.explanation: Automate testing and deployment of Airflow DAGs using GitHub Actions or GitLab CI.

github_actions.workflow:
name: Airflow DAG CI/CD

on:
  push:
    branches: [main, develop]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - name: Set up Python
        uses: actions/setup-python@v4
        with:
          python-version: '3.11'

      - name: Install dependencies
        run: |
          pip install apache-airflow==2.8.0
          pip install pytest pytest-airflow

      - name: Lint DAGs
        run: flake8 dags/ --max-line-length=120

      - name: Validate DAG syntax
        run: |
          export AIRFLOW_HOME=/tmp/airflow
          airflow db init
          python -c "from airflow.models import DagBag; d = DagBag('dags/'); assert len(d.import_errors) == 0"

      - name: Run unit tests
        run: pytest tests/ -v --tb=short

  deploy:
    needs: test
    if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main'
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Sync DAGs to S3 (MWAA)
        run: aws s3 sync dags/ s3://my-mwaa-bucket/dags/ --delete

deployment_strategies.description: Kubernetes sidecar container clones DAG repo every N seconds
deployment_strategies.config:
# values.yaml for Helm:
gitSync:
  enabled: true
  repo: https://github.com/myorg/airflow-dags
  branch: main
  subPath: dags
  period: 60s

deployment_strategies.description: CI/CD syncs DAG files to S3 bucket, MWAA auto-detects changes
DAG files baked into Docker image. Rolling deploy on new image.

topics.name: Airflow Scheduler Tuning
topics.explanation: The scheduler is the most critical component. Poor scheduler performance causes DAGs to start late or tasks to queue indefinitely.

key_settings.recommendation: 60-300 for large DAG counts. Lower = faster pickup of new DAGs but more CPU.
key_settings.description: How often each DAG file is re-parsed

key_settings.recommendation: 300 for production. Lower only when deploying DAGs frequently.
key_settings.description: How often to scan for new DAG files

key_settings.recommendation: Reduce to 256 if DB queries are slow
key_settings.description: Max task instances per scheduler DB query

scheduler_ha.description: Run multiple schedulers for high availability (Airflow 2.0+)
scheduler_ha.config:
# Start 2 schedulers — only one is active at a time (leader election):
airflow scheduler &
airflow scheduler &

topics.name: Reducing DAG Parse Time
topics.explanation: Scheduler parses every DAG file periodically. Heavy imports, DB calls, or API calls at parse time slow down the scheduler significantly.

bad_practices.code:
# BAD: DB call at parse time — runs on every scheduler heartbeat
from airflow import DAG
from myapp.db import get_client_list  # Imports DB connection at parse time

clients = get_client_list()  # DB query executed on every file parse!
for client in clients:
    with DAG(f'dag_{client}', ...) as dag:
        pass

other_tips: Use top-level code only for DAG structure definition
Use .airflowignore to exclude non-DAG Python files from scanning
Set core.min_serialized_dag_update_interval to reduce DB writes
Profile parse time: airflow dags report

optimization_example.scenario: 50 concurrent tasks needed, CeleryExecutor with 5 workers
optimization_example.config:
[core]
parallelism = 50                    # Global max tasks
max_active_tasks_per_dag = 20       # Per DAG max

[celery]
worker_concurrency = 10             # 10 tasks per worker x 5 workers = 50 total

# In specific DAGs:
DAG('high_priority', max_active_tasks=30, max_active_runs=2)
DAG('low_priority',  max_active_tasks=5,  max_active_runs=1)

topics.name: KubernetesPodOperator
topics.explanation: Runs a task in a dedicated Kubernetes Pod using any Docker image. Full environment isolation. Can use different images per task — perfect for tasks with conflicting dependencies.

example.basic:
from airflow.providers.cncf.kubernetes.operators.pod import KubernetesPodOperator
from kubernetes.client import models as k8s

run_spark_job = KubernetesPodOperator(
    task_id='run_spark_job',
    name='spark-etl-pod',
    namespace='airflow',
    image='apache/spark:3.5.0',
    cmds=['spark-submit'],
    arguments=[
        '--master', 'k8s://https://kubernetes.default.svc',
        '--class', 'com.myorg.ETLJob',
        's3://my-bucket/jobs/etl.jar',
        '--date', '{{ ds }}'
    ],
    env_vars=[
        k8s.V1EnvVar(name='S3_BUCKET',  value='my-data-lake'),
        k8s.V1EnvVar(name='EXEC_DATE',  value='{{ ds }}')
    ],
    resources=k8s.V1ResourceRequirements(
        requests={'memory': '4Gi', 'cpu': '2'},
        limits  ={'memory': '8Gi', 'cpu': '4'}
    ),
    is_delete_operator_pod=True,   # Clean up pod after completion
    get_logs=True,                 # Stream pod logs to Airflow task logs
    do_xcom_push=False,
    startup_timeout_seconds=300
)
example.with_secrets:
from airflow.providers.cncf.kubernetes.operators.pod import KubernetesPodOperator
from kubernetes.client import models as k8s

run_dbt = KubernetesPodOperator(
    task_id='run_dbt_models',
    image='my-registry/dbt:1.7.0',
    cmds=['dbt', 'run', '--select', 'tag:daily'],
    env_from=[
        k8s.V1EnvFromSource(
            secret_ref=k8s.V1SecretEnvSource(name='dbt-secrets')
        )
    ],
    volume_mounts=[
        k8s.V1VolumeMount(
            name='dbt-profiles',
            mount_path='/root/.dbt'
        )
    ],
    volumes=[
        k8s.V1Volume(
            name='dbt-profiles',
            config_map=k8s.V1ConfigMapVolumeSource(name='dbt-profiles-cm')
        )
    ]
)

advantages: Any Docker image — complete dependency isolation
Leverage Kubernetes for pod scheduling (node selectors, tolerations)
drawbacks: Pod startup overhead (30-60 seconds)
Pod failures may not report errors cleanly
Image pull latency for large images

topics.name: Airflow Helm Chart Configuration
topics.explanation: Deploy Airflow on Kubernetes using the official Helm chart from Apache Airflow.
topics.commands:
# Add Airflow Helm repo:
helm repo add apache-airflow https://airflow.apache.org
helm repo update

# Install with custom values:
helm install airflow apache-airflow/airflow \
  --namespace airflow \
  --create-namespace \
  --values values.yaml
topics.key_values_yaml:
# values.yaml
executor: KubernetesExecutor

scheduler:
  replicas: 2      # HA schedulers

webserver:
  replicas: 2
  service:
    type: LoadBalancer

data:
  metadataConnection:
    user: airflow
    pass: airflow
    host: postgres-host
    db: airflow
    port: 5432

dags:
  gitSync:
    enabled: true
    repo: https://github.com/myorg/airflow-dags
    branch: main
    subPath: dags

logs:
  persistence:
    enabled: true
    storageClassName: gp3
    size: 50Gi

extraEnv:
  - name: AIRFLOW__CORE__FERNET_KEY
    valueFrom:
      secretKeyRef:
        name: airflow-fernet-key
        key: fernet-key

topics.name: Amazon MWAA
topics.explanation: Amazon Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow. Fully managed Airflow on AWS. Integrates natively with S3 (DAGs and logs), Secrets Manager, VPC, and IAM.

setup.dag_deployment: Upload DAGs to S3: aws s3 sync dags/ s3://my-mwaa-bucket/dags/
setup.requirements: Upload requirements.txt to S3 for custom packages
setup.environment_variables: Set via MWAA environment → Airflow configuration options
setup.connections: Use Secrets Manager backend or AWS Connections (IAM-based)

drawbacks: 15-30 minute update time when changing requirements.txt or config
Limited Airflow version support (lags behind open-source)
Limited plugin support
Higher cost than self-managed for high workloads
topics.best_for: Teams wanting zero infrastructure management, AWS-native shop

topics.name: Google Cloud Composer
topics.explanation: Managed Airflow on GCP built on Google Kubernetes Engine. Deep GCP integration (BigQuery, GCS, Dataflow, Dataproc).
composer2_features: Workload Identity for GCP service auth
drawbacks: Expensive for small workloads
Long environment creation/update times (20-40 minutes)
GCP-specific provider packages pre-installed may conflict

topics.name: Astronomer
topics.explanation: Commercial Airflow platform built by Astro (the company that created Airflow). Available as SaaS (Astro Cloud) or self-hosted (Astro Software).
features: Faster deploys than MWAA/Composer (code push in seconds)
Astro CLI for local dev and deployment
Hybrid execution (run tasks in your cloud, control plane managed)
topics.astro_cli:
# Init project:
astro dev init
# Run locally:
astro dev start
# Deploy:
astro deploy

topics.name: Airflow + dbt
topics.explanation: Run dbt models from Airflow. Two approaches: DbtRunOperator from airflow-dbt-python library, or BashOperator running dbt CLI commands.

example.bash_approach:
from airflow.operators.bash import BashOperator

# Run all daily tagged models
dbt_run = BashOperator(
    task_id='dbt_run_daily',
    bash_command='''
        cd /opt/dbt/my_project && \
        dbt run \
          --profiles-dir /opt/dbt/profiles \
          --target prod \
          --select tag:daily \
          --vars '{"execution_date": "{{ ds }}"}'
    '''
)

dbt_test = BashOperator(
    task_id='dbt_test',
    bash_command='cd /opt/dbt/my_project && dbt test --select tag:daily'
)

dbt_run >> dbt_test

topics.name: Airflow + Spark
topics.explanation: Submit Spark jobs from Airflow using SparkSubmitOperator or EMR/Dataproc operators.

example.spark_submit:
from airflow.providers.apache.spark.operators.spark_submit import SparkSubmitOperator

run_spark = SparkSubmitOperator(
    task_id='run_etl_spark',
    conn_id='spark_default',
    application='s3://my-bucket/jars/etl.jar',
    name='daily_etl',
    conf={
        'spark.executor.memory': '4g',
        'spark.executor.cores': '2',
        'spark.executor.instances': '10',
        'spark.sql.shuffle.partitions': '200'
    },
    application_args=['--date', '{{ ds }}', '--env', 'prod'],
    driver_memory='2g',
    executor_memory='4g',
    num_executors=10,
    jars='s3://my-bucket/jars/dependencies.jar'
)
example.databricks:
from airflow.providers.databricks.operators.databricks import DatabricksRunNowOperator

run_notebook = DatabricksRunNowOperator(
    task_id='run_databricks_job',
    databricks_conn_id='databricks_default',
    job_id=12345,
    notebook_params={'execution_date': '{{ ds }}'}
)

example.code:
from airflow.providers.snowflake.operators.snowflake import SnowflakeOperator
from airflow.providers.snowflake.hooks.snowflake import SnowflakeHook

# Run SQL in Snowflake
load_to_snowflake = SnowflakeOperator(
    task_id='load_daily_sales',
    sql='''
        INSERT INTO analytics.gold.daily_sales
        SELECT date, region, SUM(amount) as revenue
        FROM analytics.silver.orders
        WHERE date = '{{ ds }}'
        GROUP BY date, region;
    ''',
    snowflake_conn_id='snowflake_default',
    warehouse='TRANSFORM_WH',
    database='ANALYTICS',
    schema='GOLD',
    role='TRANSFORMER'
)

# Use Hook for more control
def run_snowflake_query(**context):
    hook = SnowflakeHook(snowflake_conn_id='snowflake_default')
    results = hook.get_pandas_df(
        f"SELECT * FROM orders WHERE date = '{context['ds']}'"
    )
    return len(results)

topics.name: Data-Aware Scheduling (Datasets)
topics.explanation: Airflow 2.4+ allows scheduling DAGs based on dataset updates rather than time. When a DAG produces a dataset, downstream DAGs are triggered automatically.

example.code:
from airflow import DAG, Dataset
from airflow.operators.python import PythonOperator
from datetime import datetime

# Define datasets (logical data assets)
orders_dataset   = Dataset('s3://my-lake/silver/orders/')
customers_dataset= Dataset('s3://my-lake/silver/customers/')

# Producer DAG: declares it produces the dataset
with DAG('ingest_orders', schedule='@hourly', start_date=datetime(2024,1,1)) as producer:
    ingest = PythonOperator(
        task_id='ingest_orders',
        python_callable=ingest_fn,
        outlets=[orders_dataset]   # Marks dataset as updated when task completes
    )

# Consumer DAG: runs when BOTH datasets are updated
with DAG(
    'reporting_dag',
    schedule=[orders_dataset, customers_dataset],  # Triggered by data, not time
    start_date=datetime(2024,1,1)
) as consumer:
    report = PythonOperator(task_id='build_report', python_callable=report_fn)

benefits: True data-driven orchestration — no polling or ExternalTaskSensor needed
Clear producer-consumer relationships visible in UI
DAG runs when data is ready, not on a fixed schedule
drawbacks: Dataset-triggered DAGs don't run if no data update occurs (no time-based fallback)
Dataset URI is just a string — no actual data validation at scheduling time

topics.name: Deferrable Operators
topics.explanation: Deferrable operators suspend execution (releasing the worker slot) while waiting for an external event, then resume when the event fires. The Triggerer component handles the async waiting using asyncio. Massive resource savings for long-polling tasks.

comparison.traditional_sensor: Holds worker slot for entire wait (poke mode) or releases + re-queues (reschedule mode — still creates many short tasks)
comparison.deferrable_operator: Suspends to Triggerer (single async thread) — zero worker slots used during entire wait

example.code:
# Traditional S3 sensor uses reschedule mode — creates many task runs
# Deferrable sensor waits with zero worker slot usage:
from airflow.providers.amazon.aws.sensors.s3 import S3KeySensorAsync

wait_for_file = S3KeySensorAsync(
    task_id='wait_for_daily_file',
    bucket_name='my-bucket',
    bucket_key='data/{{ ds }}/orders.csv',
    aws_conn_id='aws_default'
    # No mode= needed — async by default
)

topics.name: Airflow REST API
topics.explanation: Airflow 2.0+ exposes a stable REST API for triggering DAGs, querying task statuses, managing connections, and more.

questions.question: What is Apache Airflow and what problem does it solve?
questions.answer: Apache Airflow is an open-source workflow orchestration platform that allows you to programmatically author, schedule, and monitor data pipelines as Python code (DAGs). It solves the problem of managing complex, multi-step pipelines that have dependencies, need retries, require scheduling, and must be monitored — replacing fragile cron jobs and shell scripts with a maintainable, version-controlled, observable system.
questions.drawbacks: Airflow is NOT a streaming tool. It has overhead per task run, making it unsuitable for sub-minute scheduling. Can become complex at very large scale.
questions.solutions: Use Airflow for orchestration only. Delegate actual data processing to Spark, dbt, or SQL. Use Kafka/Flink for streaming.

questions.question: Explain Airflow's core architecture components.
questions.answer: Airflow has five core components: (1) Webserver — serves the UI, reads metadata DB, does NOT run tasks. (2) Scheduler — parses DAG files, creates task instances, submits them to the executor. (3) Executor — determines HOW tasks run (Sequential, Local, Celery, Kubernetes). (4) Metadata Database — stores all state: DAG runs, task instances, connections, variables, XComs. (5) Workers — actually execute tasks (Celery workers or K8s pods). The Triggerer (Airflow 2.2+) handles deferrable operators asynchronously.
questions.drawbacks: Scheduler is a single point of failure in Airflow <2.0.
questions.solutions: Airflow 2.0+ supports multiple schedulers for HA. Use PostgreSQL as metadata DB (not SQLite) for production.

questions.question: What is a DAG in Airflow? How is it different from a pipeline?
questions.answer: A DAG (Directed Acyclic Graph) is a collection of tasks with dependency relationships. Directed: tasks have direction (A runs before B). Acyclic: no circular dependencies. Graph: tasks are nodes, dependencies are edges. A pipeline is a general concept; a DAG is Airflow's specific implementation. Every Airflow workflow is defined as a Python DAG object.
questions.drawbacks: DAGs are parsed periodically by the scheduler — heavy code at module level slows scheduler.
questions.solutions: Keep DAG file lightweight. Move business logic inside task callables, not at DAG parse time.

questions.question: What is the difference between execution_date and the actual run time in Airflow?
questions.answer: execution_date (now called logical_date in Airflow 2.2+) is the START of the scheduled interval, not when the task actually runs. A DAG with schedule='@daily' and start_date=2024-01-01 has its first execution_date of 2024-01-01, but it actually RUNS at 2024-01-02 00:00 (end of the interval). This is because Airflow assumes the data for a period is available AFTER the period ends.
questions.drawbacks: This 'one interval behind' behavior confuses many new Airflow users.
questions.solutions: Use {{ data_interval_start }} and {{ data_interval_end }} macros for clarity. Set catchup=False to avoid confusion during initial setup.

questions.question: What is catchup and when should you use or disable it?
questions.answer: Catchup controls whether Airflow schedules all missed DAG runs since start_date when a DAG is unpaused. If catchup=True and start_date=2024-01-01 and the DAG is activated on 2024-01-10, Airflow immediately schedules 9 past runs. Use catchup=True for historical backfilling. Use catchup=False (recommended default) when you only want future runs.
questions.drawbacks: catchup=True can flood the scheduler with hundreds of runs on first activation.
questions.solutions: Always set catchup=False by default. Use airflow dags backfill command for intentional historical reprocessing.

questions.question: Explain the difference between PythonOperator, BashOperator, and BranchPythonOperator.
questions.answer: PythonOperator: executes a Python callable function. Most flexible, best for Python logic. BashOperator: executes a bash command or script. Good for CLI tools, shell scripts, calling external programs. BranchPythonOperator: executes a Python callable that RETURNS a task_id (or list of task_ids) to run next — all other downstream branches are skipped. Used for conditional pipeline routing.
questions.drawbacks: BashOperator runs in a subprocess — environment variables and PATH may differ from expectation.
questions.solutions: Use env parameter in BashOperator to set required environment variables explicitly.

questions.question: What is XCom and what are its limitations?
questions.answer: XCom (Cross-Communication) allows tasks to share small amounts of data by pushing to and pulling from Airflow's metadata database. Return values from tasks are automatically XCom-pushed. Other tasks pull with ti.xcom_pull(task_ids='source_task'). Limitations: XCom is stored in the metadata DB — not designed for large data. PostgreSQL limit is 1GB per value but practically should be < 1KB (IDs, paths, counts). Large XComs bloat the metadata DB and cause performance issues.
questions.drawbacks: Storing large data (DataFrames, files) in XCom bloats the metadata DB and slows Airflow.
questions.solutions: Store large data in S3/GCS/database and pass only the file path via XCom. Use custom XCom backends (S3XComBackend) for larger payloads.

questions.question: What are trigger rules? Name the most commonly used ones.
questions.answer: Trigger rules define when a task should run based on upstream task states. Default is all_success (run only when all upstream succeed). Key ones: all_success (default), all_failed (run when all upstream fail), all_done (run regardless of upstream state — good for cleanup), one_success (run when at least one upstream succeeds), none_failed (run when no upstream failed — skipped is OK), none_failed_min_one_success (best for branch merging points).
questions.drawbacks: Using wrong trigger rule after BranchPythonOperator causes tasks to be unexpectedly skipped or failed.
questions.solutions: Always use none_failed_min_one_success for merge points after branching DAGs.

questions.question: What is the difference between poke mode and reschedule mode in sensors?
questions.answer: Poke mode: the sensor runs continuously on a worker, sleeping between checks. It holds the worker slot for the ENTIRE wait duration. Reschedule mode: the sensor checks the condition, then RELEASES the worker slot until the next check time. The scheduler re-queues it at the next poke interval. Reschedule mode is far more efficient for long waits — 10 sensors waiting 2 hours use 10 worker slots in poke mode but near-zero slots in reschedule mode.
questions.drawbacks: Poke mode with many long-running sensors exhausts worker slots, blocking other tasks.
questions.solutions: Default to mode='reschedule' for all sensors with poke_interval >= 60 seconds. Only use poke for very short waits (< 5 minutes).

questions.question: What is the TaskFlow API and how does it differ from traditional operators?
questions.answer: The TaskFlow API (@task decorator) is a modern Pythonic way to define Airflow tasks. It automatically handles XCom push/pull — return values are auto-pushed, function arguments auto-pull from XCom. Dependencies are inferred from function call chains. Traditional operators require explicit PythonOperator instantiation, manual XCom management, and >> dependency syntax. TaskFlow is cleaner for Python-heavy DAGs; traditional operators are still needed for non-Python operators (BashOperator, S3KeySensor, etc.).
questions.drawbacks: TaskFlow cannot be used for non-Python operators. Complex dependency graphs can be less explicit.
questions.solutions: Mix TaskFlow (@task decorated functions) with traditional operators in the same DAG.

questions.question: Compare SequentialExecutor, LocalExecutor, CeleryExecutor, and KubernetesExecutor.
questions.answer: SequentialExecutor: one task at a time, SQLite only, dev/testing only. LocalExecutor: parallel tasks using multiprocessing on one machine, requires PostgreSQL/MySQL, good for small prod. CeleryExecutor: distributes tasks across multiple worker machines via Redis/RabbitMQ queue, most popular for medium-large prod, horizontal scaling. KubernetesExecutor: each task in its own K8s pod, full environment isolation, zero idle cost, best for cloud-native large scale but has pod startup overhead (30-60s).
questions.drawbacks: CeleryExecutor: all workers need same code/deps installed, idle workers cost money. KubernetesExecutor: pod startup latency.
questions.solutions: CeleryExecutor: use Docker + git-sync for consistent worker environments. KubernetesExecutor: use for tasks > 2 minutes to amortize pod startup cost.

questions.question: How do you pass data between tasks in Airflow?
questions.answer: Three approaches: (1) XCom — small data (IDs, paths, counts) stored in metadata DB. Push via return or ti.xcom_push(). Pull via ti.xcom_pull(). (2) TaskFlow API — automatic XCom via function return values and argument injection. (3) External storage — write large data to S3/database, pass the path/reference via XCom. Third approach is recommended for any data > 1KB.
questions.drawbacks: XCom for large data bloats metadata DB, causes performance issues, and risks hitting DB size limits.
questions.solutions: Use S3/GCS for data storage. XCom only the file path: ti.xcom_push(key='path', value='s3://bucket/data.parquet')

questions.question: What are Airflow Connections and how are they managed securely?
questions.answer: Connections store external system credentials (host, port, login, password, schema) identified by conn_id. They're stored encrypted (Fernet) in the metadata DB. Secure management approaches: (1) Secrets Backend — store in AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or GCP Secret Manager. (2) Environment variables — AIRFLOW_CONN_{CONN_ID}=uri. (3) Never store in plain text or commit to Git.
questions.drawbacks: Default metadata DB storage — credentials accessible to anyone with DB access.
questions.solutions: Configure secrets backend (Vault/Secrets Manager) for production. Rotate Fernet key periodically.

questions.question: What is the Scheduler and what happens if it goes down?
questions.answer: The Scheduler is the heartbeat of Airflow — it parses DAG files, creates DAGRuns, schedules task instances, and submits them to the executor. If the scheduler goes down, no new tasks are scheduled or submitted. Running tasks continue (they're on workers), but new tasks won't start. Airflow 2.0+ supports multiple schedulers (HA) — secondary scheduler takes over if primary dies.
questions.drawbacks: Single scheduler is a SPOF in older Airflow. High DAG count slows scheduler parse cycle.
questions.solutions: Run 2+ schedulers (Airflow 2.0+). Reduce DAG file parse time. Use PostgreSQL as metadata DB. Monitor scheduler heartbeat.

questions.question: What is depends_on_past and when would you use it?
questions.answer: depends_on_past=True means a task instance will only run if the PREVIOUS run of the same task succeeded. Useful for incremental processing where today's run must not start if yesterday's failed — for example, an incremental database load where each day builds on the previous.
questions.drawbacks: If one day fails and is not fixed, ALL future runs of that task are blocked indefinitely.
questions.solutions: Monitor for depends_on_past blocks. Fix failing runs and mark as success, or clear them for retry. Add alerting on long-queued task instances.

questions.question: How do you implement branching in Airflow?
questions.answer: Use BranchPythonOperator or @task.branch. The callable returns the task_id (or list of task_ids) to execute next. All other branches are SKIPPED. At the merge point after branching, use trigger_rule='none_failed_min_one_success' so the merge task runs when at least one branch succeeds and none failed (handles skipped branches correctly).
questions.drawbacks: Without correct trigger_rule at merge point, the merge task may be skipped even if a branch succeeded.
questions.solutions: Always set trigger_rule='none_failed_min_one_success' on tasks immediately after a branch convergence point.

questions.question: What are Pools in Airflow and when would you use them?
questions.answer: Pools limit the number of concurrently running tasks accessing a shared resource. Each pool has a fixed number of slots. Tasks in a pool use one slot (or more with pool_slots parameter) while running. When pool is full, tasks queue. Use pools to: limit concurrent DB connections, rate-limit API calls, prevent resource overload on shared systems.
questions.drawbacks: Pool exhaustion silently queues tasks — without monitoring, you may not notice tasks waiting for slots.
questions.solutions: Monitor pool utilization via Airflow metrics (airflow.pool.queued_slots). Alert when queued slots > threshold. Set task timeout to prevent tasks holding slots indefinitely.

questions.question: How do you dynamically generate DAGs in Airflow?
questions.answer: Dynamically generate DAGs by writing Python code that creates DAG objects in a loop and registers them in globals(). Common pattern: read a config file (YAML/JSON) or list, create one DAG per item, assign each to globals()[dag_id]. Airflow discovers all DAG objects in the global namespace at parse time.
questions.drawbacks: Too many dynamic DAGs (> 500) slow down scheduler parsing. Config read at parse time (DB query, API call) runs on every heartbeat.
questions.solutions: Read from static config file, not DB/API. Limit dynamic DAG count. Consider Dynamic Task Mapping (.expand()) as an alternative for same-structure pipelines.

questions.question: What is Dynamic Task Mapping (.expand()) and how does it differ from dynamic DAGs?
questions.answer: Dynamic Task Mapping (.expand()) creates multiple instances of the SAME task at runtime based on a list of inputs — no need to know task count at write time. Dynamic DAGs create multiple separate DAG objects at parse time. Use .expand() when: same pipeline structure, different inputs (files, tables, clients). Use dynamic DAGs when: fundamentally different schedules or structures per item.
questions.drawbacks: Mapped task count not known until runtime — UI shows placeholder until first run.
questions.solutions: Monitor mapped task instances in UI under the parent task. Set pool limits to prevent mapped tasks from overwhelming resources.

questions.question: How do you implement SLA monitoring in Airflow?
questions.answer: Set sla parameter on tasks or in default_args (timedelta). Set sla_miss_callback on the DAG. The callback fires when a task hasn't completed within its SLA window (measured from DAG execution_date). The callback receives the list of missed tasks and blocking tasks — use it to send Slack/PagerDuty alerts.
questions.drawbacks: SLA miss does NOT stop the task. SLA email sent only once. SLA measured from execution_date not task start time.
questions.solutions: Use callbacks to send rich alerts to Slack/PagerDuty. Combine SLA with task-level on_failure_callback for comprehensive monitoring.

questions.question: What is a Hook in Airflow? How is it different from an Operator?
questions.answer: A Hook is an interface to an external system (PostgresHook, S3Hook, SnowflakeHook). It handles authentication and connection details using a conn_id. An Operator defines a TASK — what work to do. Operators internally use Hooks to connect to external systems. You use Hooks directly when you need more control inside a PythonOperator or custom operator than a pre-built operator provides.
questions.drawbacks: None significant. Hooks are the correct abstraction for connection management.
questions.solutions: Prefer Hooks inside custom operators over hardcoding credentials. Always use conn_id pattern.

questions.question: How do you test Airflow DAGs?
questions.answer: Three levels: (1) Structure tests — use DagBag to load DAGs and assert task count, schedule, dependencies, retries, catchup settings. (2) Unit tests — test Python callables independently with mock connections/hooks (unittest.mock). (3) Integration tests — run actual DAG in a test environment with test database. CI/CD: run structure + unit tests on every PR, validate DAG syntax (DagBag import errors = 0).
questions.drawbacks: Full integration tests are slow and require external systems.
questions.solutions: Run structure + unit tests in CI. Reserve integration tests for staging environment before prod deployment.

questions.question: What causes DAG parsing to be slow and how do you fix it?
questions.answer: Slow DAG parsing causes: (1) Database queries or API calls at module level (run on every parse cycle). (2) Heavy library imports at top of DAG file. (3) Too many DAG files. (4) Complex DAG structure with thousands of tasks. Fixes: Move all heavy logic inside callable functions. Cache config reads from static files. Use .airflowignore to exclude non-DAG files. Increase min_file_process_interval to parse less frequently. Profile with airflow dags report.
questions.drawbacks: Slow parsing causes scheduler lag — tasks start late even if all workers are available.
questions.solutions: Keep DAG file < 1 second to parse. Test parse time: time python dags/my_dag.py

questions.question: What is the ExternalTaskSensor and what are its limitations?
questions.answer: ExternalTaskSensor waits for a task (or entire DAG) in ANOTHER DAG to reach a specific state (success by default). Used for cross-DAG dependencies. It matches execution dates using execution_delta or execution_date_fn. Limitations: tightly couples two DAGs by string dag_id/task_id. Requires exact schedule alignment or careful execution_delta configuration. Sensor holds worker slot (use reschedule mode).
questions.drawbacks: If upstream DAG is renamed, sensor fails. Misaligned schedules cause sensor to wait forever.
questions.solutions: Use check_existence=True to fail fast on missing upstream DAG. Always use mode='reschedule'. Consider Datasets (Airflow 2.4+) for a cleaner data-driven dependency model.

questions.question: What are deferrable operators and how do they save resources?
questions.answer: Deferrable operators (Airflow 2.2+) suspend execution and release the worker slot while waiting for an external event. The Triggerer component handles async waiting using asyncio.
