Traditional sensors in reschedule mode still create scheduler work (re-queue every N seconds). Deferrable operators: zero worker slots + minimal Triggerer resources during wait. One Triggerer can handle thousands of deferred tasks concurrently.
questions.drawbacks: Requires Triggerer component running. Not all operators have deferrable versions yet.
questions.solutions: Use *Async variants (S3KeySensorAsync, HttpSensorAsync) where available. Ensure Triggerer is deployed (included in Helm chart and Docker Compose by default in 2.x).

questions.question: How do you handle secrets and credentials securely in Airflow?
questions.answer: Three-tier approach: (1) Secrets Backend — configure AWS Secrets Manager, HashiCorp Vault, or GCP Secret Manager as the secrets backend. Airflow fetches credentials at runtime, never stores in metadata DB. (2) Environment variables — AIRFLOW_CONN_{CONN_ID} for connections, AIRFLOW__CORE__FERNET_KEY for encryption. Inject via Kubernetes Secrets or Docker secrets. (3) Never hardcode credentials in DAG files or commit to Git.
questions.drawbacks: Metadata DB stores encrypted connections — risk if DB is compromised and Fernet key is exposed.
questions.solutions: Use secrets backend (Vault/Secrets Manager) so credentials never touch Airflow's DB. Rotate Fernet key regularly. Use least-privilege IAM roles instead of static credentials where possible.

questions.question: What is Jinja templating in Airflow and what are template_fields?
questions.answer: Jinja templating lets you inject dynamic values (execution date, variables, XCom values, connections) into task parameters using {{ }} syntax. Templates are rendered at TASK EXECUTION time, not DAG parse time. template_fields is a tuple of attribute names on an operator that support Jinja rendering. Built-in macros: {{ ds }}, {{ ts }}, {{ run_id }}, {{ var.value.key }}, {{ ti.xcom_pull('task_id') }}.
questions.drawbacks: Templates evaluated at execution time — syntax errors only caught at runtime, not parse time.
questions.solutions: Test template rendering: airflow tasks render dag_id task_id execution_date. Add template syntax validation to CI pipeline.

questions.question: What is the difference between SubDAGs and TaskGroups?
questions.answer: SubDAGs (deprecated): create a child DAG object that appears as a single task in the parent. Uses SubDagOperator which spawns a separate scheduler process. Causes deadlocks with CeleryExecutor (child tasks use parent pool slots). TaskGroups (recommended): purely visual grouping in the same DAG. No separate scheduler, no deadlock risk, no performance overhead. Both provide visual organization in the UI — TaskGroups do it better.
questions.drawbacks: SubDAGs: deadlocks, complex debugging, scheduler overhead. TaskGroups: no equivalent drill-down UI as SubDAGs had.
questions.solutions: Migrate all SubDAGs to TaskGroups. TaskGroups collapse/expand in UI and have no operational downsides.

questions.question: How do you implement retry logic in Airflow?
questions.answer: Set retries (number of retry attempts) and retry_delay (timedelta between retries) in default_args or per-task. Optional: retry_exponential_backoff=True for exponential backoff. on_retry_callback for alerting on each retry. Retries create new task instance attempts (visible in UI). The task is marked as success if any retry succeeds.
questions.drawbacks: Retries can mask underlying systemic issues if retry count is too high.
questions.solutions: Set reasonable retries (2-3). Use retry_exponential_backoff=True to avoid hammering recovering systems. Alert on retry via on_retry_callback. Investigate root cause when retries occur frequently.

questions.question: What is the Airflow metadata database and what is stored in it?
questions.answer: The metadata database is the central state store for Airflow. It stores: DAG definitions and structure, DAGRun records (scheduled/manual runs), TaskInstance records (state, start/end time, log location), XCom values, Connections (encrypted), Variables (encrypted), Pools and slot counts, User accounts and RBAC roles, SLA miss records. PostgreSQL is recommended for production (SQLite for dev only).
questions.drawbacks: Metadata DB becomes a bottleneck and SPOF. Large XComs and log metadata bloat the DB over time.
questions.solutions: Use PostgreSQL with connection pooling (PgBouncer). Schedule regular DB cleanup: airflow db clean. Store logs externally (S3) not in DB. Use remote logging.

questions.question: How does Airflow handle task failures? What happens to downstream tasks?
questions.answer: When a task fails: (1) If retries > 0, task is retried after retry_delay. (2) If all retries exhausted, task is marked FAILED. (3) All downstream tasks that depend on this task are marked UPSTREAM_FAILED (shown in UI). (4) on_failure_callback fires on each failure. (5) DAGRun is marked FAILED. Downstream tasks are NOT marked FAILED — they're UPSTREAM_FAILED, which is distinct. You can manually mark failed tasks as success to unblock downstream.
questions.drawbacks: UPSTREAM_FAILED downstream tasks can be misleading — they didn't fail, they just couldn't run.
questions.solutions: Use clear task (mark for re-run) to retry failed pipelines. Use trigger_rule='all_done' for cleanup tasks that should run even on failure.

questions.question: How do you implement CI/CD for Airflow DAGs?
questions.answer: CI pipeline: (1) On PR: lint (flake8/black), run DAG structure tests (DagBag), run unit tests (pytest). (2) On merge to main: sync DAGs to target environment (S3 for MWAA, git-sync for K8s, Docker image for custom). CD strategies: git-sync sidecar (K8s pulls from Git repo), S3 sync (MWAA), Astro CLI deploy (Astronomer), baked Docker image with rolling deploy.
questions.drawbacks: Forgetting to test DAGs before deploying can break production pipelines silently.
questions.solutions: Make CI mandatory (block PR merge without passing tests). Include DAG import validation: assert len(DagBag().import_errors) == 0.

questions.question: What is the KubernetesPodOperator and when would you use it?
questions.answer: KubernetesPodOperator launches a Kubernetes Pod to execute a task using any Docker image. Used when: a task needs different Python/library version than the Airflow base image, task needs heavy resources (GPU, large memory), complete environment isolation is required (different dependencies per task team). The pod is created on task start and deleted on completion.
questions.drawbacks: Pod startup overhead (30-60 seconds). Pod scheduling failures may not report errors cleanly to Airflow.
questions.solutions: Not suitable for tasks < 2 minutes (overhead > execution time). Pre-pull images on nodes. Set startup_timeout_seconds appropriately. Always set is_delete_operator_pod=True to clean up.

questions.question: What are Datasets in Airflow and how do they enable data-aware scheduling?
questions.answer: Datasets (Airflow 2.4+) are logical representations of data assets (S3 paths, database tables). Producer tasks declare outlets=[my_dataset] — when they complete, the dataset is marked updated. Consumer DAGs declare schedule=[my_dataset] — they run when the dataset is updated. This enables true data-driven orchestration: downstream DAGs trigger on data availability, not time.
questions.drawbacks: Dataset URI is just a string — no actual data validation. Consumer DAG won't run if producer never updates the dataset (no time-based fallback by default).
questions.solutions: Combine with time-based schedule fallback for critical pipelines. Use meaningful URIs that match actual data locations.

questions.question: How do you monitor Airflow in production?
questions.answer: Four monitoring layers: (1) Airflow UI — DAG/task status, task logs, SLA misses. (2) StatsD/Prometheus metrics — executor slots, queued tasks, scheduler heartbeat, parse times. (3) External alerting — Slack/PagerDuty via on_failure_callback and sla_miss_callback. (4) Log aggregation — ship Airflow logs to CloudWatch/ELK for analysis. Key metrics to alert: scheduler heartbeat gap > 30s, executor queued tasks > threshold, pool queued slots > 0 sustained.
questions.drawbacks: Out-of-box monitoring is limited. No built-in Slack/PagerDuty integration.
questions.solutions: Deploy prometheus-statsd-exporter + Grafana dashboards. Implement on_failure_callback on all production DAGs.

questions.question: How would you design a daily ETL pipeline in Airflow?
questions.answer: Design: (1) Set schedule='0 2 * * *', catchup=False, max_active_runs=1. (2) S3KeySensor or ExternalTaskSensor to wait for source data. (3) PythonOperator/KubernetesPodOperator for extraction to raw S3. (4) SparkSubmitOperator or DatabricksRunNowOperator for transformation. (5) SnowflakeOperator for loading. (6) Data quality checks (Great Expectations). (7) Notification task on success. Use depends_on_past=False, retries=3, retry_delay=5min, on_failure_callback for Slack alerts.
questions.drawbacks: Hard dependencies between tasks mean one failure blocks entire pipeline.
questions.solutions: Add data quality gates between layers. Use ShortCircuitOperator to skip downstream if source data is empty. Implement idempotent tasks for safe reruns.

questions.question: What is the Triggerer and how does it work?
questions.answer: The Triggerer is an Airflow component (2.2+) that manages deferrable operators. When a task raises TaskDeferred exception, it moves to DEFERRED state and registers a Trigger with the Triggerer. The Triggerer runs all triggers asynchronously using Python asyncio in a single thread. When a trigger fires (e.g., S3 file appears), the Triggerer marks the task for resumption and the scheduler re-queues it to a worker. One Triggerer handles thousands of deferred tasks with minimal resource usage.
questions.drawbacks: Triggerer is a new component — must be deployed separately. Not all operators have deferrable versions.
questions.solutions: Include Triggerer in all Airflow deployments (it's in official Helm chart). Migrate to *Async sensor variants where available.

questions.question: What is Amazon MWAA and how does it differ from self-managed Airflow?
questions.answer: Amazon MWAA (Managed Workflows for Apache Airflow) is a fully managed Airflow service on AWS. AWS handles infrastructure, scaling, patching, and HA. DAGs deployed via S3 sync. Connections via Secrets Manager. Differences: MWAA lags behind Airflow versions (1-2 releases), takes 15-30 minutes to update requirements.txt, limited executor customization, higher cost than self-managed at scale. Benefits: zero infrastructure management, AWS IAM integration, native S3/Glue/Redshift connectivity.
questions.drawbacks: Slow update cycles, limited to AWS, higher per-task cost at high scale.
questions.solutions: Use MWAA for AWS-native teams wanting zero ops overhead. Use Astronomer or self-managed K8s for more control.

questions.question: How do you handle idempotency in Airflow tasks?
questions.answer: Idempotent tasks produce the same result whether run once or multiple times. Implement by: (1) UPSERT instead of INSERT for DB writes. (2) Write to deterministic S3 paths (date-based) that overwrite on rerun. (3) DELETE + INSERT for partition-based tables. (4) Use execution_date in output paths to isolate each run. (5) Check if output already exists before processing.
questions.drawbacks: Non-idempotent tasks cause duplicate data on manual reruns or backfills.
questions.solutions: Design every task to be safely re-runnable. Test idempotency by triggering the same DAG run twice and verifying identical output.

questions.question: What is the difference between max_active_runs and max_active_tasks in Airflow?
questions.answer: max_active_runs: maximum number of CONCURRENT DAG RUNS (entire DAG executions). E.g., max_active_runs=1 ensures only one run of a DAG executes at a time — new runs queue until current finishes. max_active_tasks: maximum number of CONCURRENT TASKS within a single DAG run. E.g., max_active_tasks=5 means at most 5 tasks from this DAG run at the same time. Both are set at DAG level and can be set globally in airflow.cfg.
questions.drawbacks: Setting max_active_runs=1 means slow DAG runs block all future scheduled runs from starting.
questions.solutions: Set max_active_runs=1 for pipelines that process partitioned data (prevents race conditions). Set higher for independent parallel pipelines.

questions.question: How do you implement alerting in Airflow?
questions.answer: Multiple levels: (1) Task level — on_failure_callback, on_retry_callback, on_success_callback per task or in default_args. (2) DAG level — sla_miss_callback for SLA violations. (3) Email alerts — email_on_failure=True, email_on_retry=True in default_args with SMTP configured. (4) Slack — implement callback that posts to Slack webhook. (5) PagerDuty — callback that creates PagerDuty incident. (6) Metrics — Prometheus alerts on executor slot exhaustion, scheduler heartbeat.
questions.drawbacks: Email alerts from Airflow require SMTP server configuration. Callback errors can silently fail.
questions.solutions: Wrap callback bodies in try/except. Test callbacks in staging. Use multiple alert channels (Slack primary + email backup).

questions.question: What is Fernet encryption in Airflow?
questions.answer: Fernet is symmetric encryption used by Airflow to encrypt Connection passwords and Variable values before storing in the metadata DB. Generated key: python -c "from cryptography.fernet import Fernet; print(Fernet.generate_key().decode())". Set via AIRFLOW__CORE__FERNET_KEY environment variable or airflow.cfg. Without a Fernet key, Airflow stores credentials in plaintext in the DB.
questions.drawbacks: If Fernet key is lost, all encrypted connections/variables are unrecoverable. Same key must be distributed to all Airflow instances.
questions.solutions: Store Fernet key in AWS Secrets Manager or Vault. Rotate periodically with airflow rotate-fernet-key. Never commit to Git.

questions.question: Explain how Airflow Connections work with Hooks and Operators.
questions.answer: Connections store external system credentials (host, port, login, password, extra JSON). Hooks use a conn_id to fetch the Connection and establish the actual connection (e.g., PostgresHook gets conn → creates psycopg2 connection). Operators use Hooks internally to interact with external systems. Users interact with Connections (set them up once), write code using Operators/Hooks (reference conn_id). This abstraction means changing credentials only requires updating the Connection, not code.
questions.drawbacks: conn_id strings hardcoded in DAG code couple DAGs to specific connection names.
questions.solutions: Standardize conn_id naming conventions. Use environment-specific conn_ids or override via environment variables per deployment.

questions.question: How do you scale Airflow for high-volume production workloads?
questions.answer: Scaling approach: (1) Executor: use CeleryExecutor (horizontal worker scaling) or KubernetesExecutor (pod-per-task). (2) Scheduler: run 2+ schedulers for HA (Airflow 2.0+). (3) Metadata DB: use PostgreSQL with PgBouncer connection pooling. (4) Reduce parse time: optimize DAG files, increase min_file_process_interval. (5) Pools: prevent resource overload. (6) Task concurrency tuning: set parallelism, worker_concurrency, max_active_tasks.
questions.drawbacks: Scaling metadata DB is often the true bottleneck — all workers read/write DB constantly.
questions.solutions: Add PgBouncer in front of PostgreSQL. Schedule DB cleanup regularly. Consider remote logging (S3) to reduce DB writes.

questions.question: What is git-sync and how is it used for DAG deployment?
questions.answer: Git-sync is a Kubernetes sidecar container that continuously clones/pulls a Git repository to a shared volume. In Airflow K8s deployments, git-sync runs alongside the scheduler and webserver, pulling DAG files from Git every N seconds. Airflow reads DAGs from the shared volume. New DAG commits appear in Airflow within seconds of git push.
questions.drawbacks: DAG updates take N seconds (sync interval) to appear — not truly instant. Syntax errors in any DAG file block the entire directory parse.
questions.solutions: Run DAG validation in CI before merging. Set git-sync period to 30-60 seconds for reasonable freshness. Combine with CI/CD for validation before auto-deploy.

questions.question: How do you integrate Airflow with dbt?
questions.answer: Three approaches: (1) BashOperator running dbt CLI commands directly (simplest, dbt installed in Airflow env). (2) KubernetesPodOperator running dbt in a dedicated dbt Docker image (clean environment isolation). (3) DbtCloudRunJobOperator from apache-airflow-providers-dbt-cloud (trigger dbt Cloud jobs via API, wait for completion). For granular task-level visibility, use cosmos (Astronomer's dbt-airflow integration) which creates one Airflow task per dbt model.
questions.drawbacks: BashOperator approach: dbt must be installed in Airflow environment — dependency conflicts possible. dbt Cloud approach: requires dbt Cloud subscription.
questions.solutions: Use KubernetesPodOperator with dbt image for clean isolation. Use Astronomer Cosmos for per-model task visibility and dependency mapping in Airflow UI.

questions.question: What are the common causes of Airflow performance issues?
questions.answer: Top causes: (1) Slow DAG parsing — DB/API calls at module level, heavy imports. (2) Metadata DB bottleneck — too many task instances, XCom bloat, insufficient DB resources. (3) Executor slot exhaustion — parallelism set too low, pool exhaustion. (4) Scheduler lag — too many DAG files, slow parse interval. (5) Worker OOM — tasks requesting too much memory on shared workers. (6) Too many short tasks — scheduler/DB overhead per task exceeds execution time.
questions.drawbacks: Performance issues compound — slow scheduler causes cascading delays across all pipelines.
questions.solutions: Profile DAG parse time. Schedule DB cleanup. Use PgBouncer. Monitor StatsD metrics. Batch very short tasks into fewer larger tasks.

questions.question: What are Airflow Variables and what are their performance implications?
questions.answer: Variables are key-value pairs stored in the metadata DB. Problem: Variable.get() at DAG parse time (module level) executes a DB query on EVERY scheduler parse cycle (every 30 seconds by default). With 100 DAG files each calling Variable.get() = 100+ DB queries every 30 seconds just for variable reads.
questions.drawbacks: Variable.get() at module/parse level creates excessive DB load and slows the scheduler significantly.
questions.solutions: Access Variables INSIDE task callables (not at module level). Use Jinja templates {{ var.value.key }} which evaluate at execution time. Cache Variable values in os.environ if needed at startup.

questions.question: How would you debug a task that is stuck in 'running' state?
questions.answer: Debugging steps: (1) Check task logs in UI for last log output — process may be hanging or waiting. (2) Check worker machine/pod — is the process still running? (ps aux | grep airflow). (3) Check for DB locks if task is doing DB operations. (4) Check network connectivity from worker to external systems. (5) If process died externally (OOM kill, node restart), task stays 'running' in DB — use airflow tasks clear to reset. (6) Check zombie task detection: scheduler should detect and fail orphaned tasks.
questions.drawbacks: Zombie tasks (worker died but task shows running) block downstream and use phantom pool slots.
questions.solutions: Set task execution_timeout on long-running tasks. Enable zombie task detection (scheduler.zombie_threshold_in_seconds). Use process supervision (supervisord, K8s restartPolicy) to restart crashed workers.

questions.question: Design a real-world data platform orchestration using Airflow.
questions.answer: Architecture for a daily analytics platform: (1) S3KeySensor waits for vendor files to arrive in S3 (reschedule mode, poke every 5 min, timeout 4 hours). (2) KubernetesPodOperator runs Spark job to ingest raw data to Bronze Delta layer. (3) DatabricksRunNowOperator triggers dbt/Databricks job for Silver layer transformation. (4) SnowflakeOperator runs aggregation SQL for Gold layer. (5) Great Expectations task for data quality validation. (6) ShortCircuitOperator skips downstream if quality fails. (7) TriggerDagRunOperator triggers reporting DAG. (8) SlackOperator sends success notification. All tasks: retries=3, retry_delay=5min, on_failure_callback=slack_alert, pool='compute_pool' (limit 10 concurrent jobs), max_active_runs=1.
questions.drawbacks: Multi-system orchestration creates many failure points and tightly couples Airflow to each system's availability.
questions.solutions: Implement circuit-breaker pattern via ShortCircuitOperator. Use idempotent writes at every layer. Store intermediate results with execution_date in path for isolation. Add observability at each layer with Great Expectations.
